title_en: Zakat and Fasting in Nayl al-Awtar
content_en:
Ash-Shawkani's zakat and fasting chapters in Nayl al-Awtar follow the same hadith-centered comparative method: for each ruling, he presents the relevant hadiths, assesses their authenticity, presents the four schools' positions, and identifies the evidentially strongest view — which he sometimes explicitly states and sometimes leaves to the reader to deduce from the hadith assessment.

The foundational hadith for the nisab of gold and silver — 'There is no zakat on less than five uqiyyas' (of silver) and 'There is no zakat on less than twenty mithqals' (of gold) (al-Bukhari, Muslim) — is assessed as definitively authentic. These hadiths establish the nisab thresholds accepted by all four schools, representing one of the areas of universal scholarly agreement in zakat law.

For agricultural zakat, the foundational hadith is: 'On rain-watered crops and springs, a tenth; on irrigated crops, a twentieth' (al-Bukhari). Ash-Shawkani assesses this as sahih and notes that while all four schools accept this rate differential, they differ on which crops are covered. He engages the Hanafi argument for broader coverage (all agricultural produce) against the Shafi'i and Maliki restriction to storable staple foods, ultimately leaning toward the broader view as more consistent with the hadith's unrestricted language.

On the question of zakat on gold jewelry worn by women, ash-Shawkani presents the competing hadith evidence. On one side: hadiths of 'A'ishah and other Companions indicating that women's jewelry was not subject to zakat. On the other side: hadiths reporting the Prophet telling women wearing gold jewelry to pay zakat on it. He assesses the pro-zakat hadiths as stronger, aligning with the Hanbali and Hanafi positions and against the Shafi'i and Maliki positions exempting women's jewelry. This is one of ash-Shawkani's clearest departures from the majority position.

For Ramadan fasting, ash-Shawkani presents the definitive hadith: 'Fast upon sighting it [the new crescent] and break the fast upon sighting it; if it is obscured from you, complete thirty days of Sha'ban' (al-Bukhari, Muslim). He uses this hadith to establish that the Ramadan obligation begins with moon sighting, and that astronomical calculation cannot substitute for visual sighting as the trigger for the obligation — a position consistent with all four classical schools.

The chapter on what breaks the fast addresses the hadiths on cupping (hijamah) with particular care, given the Hanbali school's distinctive position that cupping breaks the fast. The hadith 'The cupper and the one cupped have broken their fast' (Ahmad, Abu Dawud) is assessed by ash-Shawkani as authentic. He then engages the arguments of those who claim this ruling was later abrogated by the report that the Prophet permitted cupping for a fasting person. His conclusion — that the abrogation claim is not strongly established — leads him to support the Hanbali view that cupping breaks the fast, departing from the Hanafi, Maliki, and Shafi'i consensus.
